### Post by HungSquirrel on 2004-10-15

ECS K7S5A motherboard
AMD AthlonXP 1600+ 1.4GHz overclocked to ~1.6GHz
512MB DDR-RAM
80GB Western Digital hard drive for OSs
60GB IBM hard drive for storage
ATI Radeon 9800 that thankfully was easy to get working in Ubuntu

My Yoper box is an ancient IBM ThinkPad 390X with:
Pentium III 500MHz
192MB SDRAM
12GB hard disk


I use Firefox exclusively on any OS it can run on.  In the event I am forced to use a computer lab machine without it, I just [bring it with me](http://www.collegechixors.com/index.php?p=63).

### Post by emperor on 2004-10-21
We have ** 5 computers** operational in our house right now, ALL running Linux!  My wife and son still work in Windose about 10% of the time due to education requirements! Myself and my daughter boot linux!

1, **Nfs-Samba File Server** -  AMD Athlon XP 1800, 256MB, 30GB &amp; 60GB ATA100 drives,  RH9 Linux.

2. **Emperor's Com-put-tor** - AMD Athlon XP 1700, 256MB, 160GB &amp; 30 GB ATA drives, Multi-boot - Win98, Win2000, RH9, FC1, FC2, ArchLinux, Ubuntu  (set to auto-boot to Ubuntu!)

3. **Wife's Computer** - Dell Inspiron 1000 - Celeron 2 Ghz, 256MB, 30GB ATA100 drive,  Dual Boot - WinXP Home &amp; FC2.

4. **Son's Computer** - AMD Athlon XP 1700, 256MB, 60GB ATA100 drive, Dual-Boot - Win2000 &amp; FC2

5.**Daughter's Computer** (she is 12!) - AMD Sampron 2200, 256MB, 20GB ATA100 drive, Ubuntu Liinux only!

Built all machines except, of course, the Dell Laptop! Have wired and wireless ethernet home network installed! Desktop screenshots can be seen on my website! Some would say I am having too much fun!  :D

### Post by rabeldable on 2005-11-08
For the record:

I have Ubuntu 5.10 installed and functioning on a Toshiba M65-S9065.  I tried Ubuntu 4.10 with no luck, the install could not see my hard drive.  I also tried to install Fedora Core 4 with no luck as X would not load and FC just hangs to the point that I need to do a hard reboot.  Solaris 10 x86 did not do any better and just to see I tried RHEL4.0.  Still no X, but I had a working system if I wanted my laptop to be a server.  Anyways, I downloaded Ubuntu 5.10 and it works great.  I have officially replaced my old HP laptop running windowz with a nice new Toshiba, 1.7Ghz CPU, 512MB RAM, CDRW/DVDRW, 17 inch wide screen 1400x900 display with wireless support running Breezy Badger! 

It literally took all night to get everything to work, however, it works.  Here are a few words of advise.

- do not worry about wireless configuration at bootup, it will not work.
    - install using the onboard NIC and after the system automatically updates go to system->admin->Networking and configure your wireless interface.  Deactivate the eth0 device and reboot.

- when setting up X ctrl-alt-backspace is your friend, use it to go back to the command line when the screen does not load.  

Follow these steps as user root in the same order to setup X:
     - get and run a program called 915resolution (search google for examples)
     - run the following command to reconfigure your X system:
                # dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
                      - I used the Frame Buffer device (i'm not gaming and I don't plan on watching dvd's so this should be fine for me)

                              (update:  I just watched the last samurai, only problem is the sound goes away when I do full screen, however, Ogle allows for maximize window to the entire screen minus the top and bottom icon/tool bars)

     - run the command 'startx'

To have X start at system boot, simply echo the 915resolution command with the full path into the following file /etc/init.d/res-fix
    
   here is my configuration:
 # echo "/full/path/to/915resolution 3c 1400 900" > /etc/init.d/res-fix

Change permissions for execute:
 # chmod u+x /etc/init.d/res-fix

Link the program to all of your rc directories, before gdm starts
 # for scriptdir in 1 2 3 4 5 6; do ln -s /etc/init.d/res-fix /etc/rc.$scriptdir.d/S12res-fix;done

(at this point I do not know of a different way to enable a service in ubuntu)

Test it by rebooting, and use caution with the hibernate functionality.  If all else fails, get back to a command line and do a fresh reboot.

For those that are interested here is my xorg.conf:

################################################################
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/CID"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
        # paths to defoma fonts
	FontPath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
	FontPath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"GLcore"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"
	Load	"int10"
	Load	"type1"
	Load	"vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc104"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"		"ImPS/2"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Synaptics Touchpad"
	Driver		"synaptics"
	Option		"SendCoreEvents"	"true"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/psaux"
	Option		"Protocol"		"auto-dev"
	Option		"HorizScrollDelta"	"0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Intel Corporation Intel Default Card"
	Driver		"i810"
	BusID		"PCI:0:2:0"
	VideoRam	131072
	Option		"UseFBDev"		"true"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Monitor0"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Intel Corporation Intel Default Card"
	Monitor		"Monitor0"
	DefaultDepth	16
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1440x900" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		4
		Modes		"1440x900" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		8
		Modes		"1440x900" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		15
		Modes		"1440x900" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		16
		Modes		"1440x900" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		24
		Modes		"1440x900" "1280x768" "1200x800" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
	Screen		"Default Screen"
	InputDevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice	"Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice	"Synaptics Touchpad"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

#####################################################


I hope this helps someone.

David

---

it's a decent setup, I keep all my windows games on my linux HDD and vice versa. nice stable etc. built it myself. Was on a budget though so I could pretty much only get the cheapest parts on sale, the two HDDs just kind of happened(I thought one was dead but was wrong)

### Post by angrykeyboarder on 2006-01-12
[quote=Iandefor]My new computer finally got built! Let there be dancing in the streets! Here's a snapshot of my system specs:

1 GB Corsair Valueselect memory

I'm sure they love the [free advertising]("http://yahoo.businessweek.com/technology/content/nov2005/tc20051118_333155.htm") as well.  I've found *other* retailers who offer comprarable prices (e.g. [Page Computers]("http://newsite.pagecomputers.com/store/Default.asp"), [Provantage]("http://www.provantage.com/"), [Buy.com]("http://www.buy.com"), to name a few).  Newegg's blg plus is the consumer ratings/comments area for each product they sell.

Unfortunatly [they are part of the same company]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newegg") who owns [ABS Computers]("http://www.labbb.org/BBBWeb/Forms/Business/CompanyReportPage_Expository.aspx?CompanyID=92002302").  I bought my current PC from [ABS.]("http://www.labbb.org/BBBWeb/Forms/Business/CompanyReportPage_Expository.aspx?CompanyID=92002302")  And to put it briefly, [I will ***never*** buy from them again]("http://tinyurl.com/a72bl").  

Therefore I have no desire to support their company in any way. They stink.

### Post by MetalMusicAddict on 2006-02-11
Heres some of the new server Im workin on. I removed 2 5" and floppy drive bays to add more HD space. I have 5 now but I will add 2 more when they get back from RMA.
I also added a second front 120mm fan (still need to install the exhaust one). I also have round IDE cables comming.

In the end this is WAY more power than my home server needs to be. My old 950mhz AMD went up (mobo actually) so when I got new cheap parts it was still alot of power.

**Desktop** - dual boots Windows and Ubuntu

**Music machine** - runs Windows and Gentoo
Crap with great audio (MSI whatever motherboard, Athlon 900, 512MB RAM, RAID 40+40GB +30GB storage, TNT2 video but most importantly an [ST Audio C-Port](http://www.staudio.com) soundcard with 19" rack for IO - 8 analog in + 8 analog out, 2 digital in + 2 digital out)

**Server 1** - runs OpenBSD SPARC64 (patch branch)
Sun Ultra10 (333MHZ 2MB cache SPARC64, 80GB IDE disk, 128MB RAM)

**Server 2** - runs stock Debian stable
Dell Poweredge 1400SC (2x800MHz Coppermine, 512MB registered ECC RAM, all SCSI disk subsystem baby! ...and it whines like an F-15 taking off!)

I'm thinking about upgrading my system to a 3.0 GHz Celeron and some better video card, as an Ati... does anyone can tell me if this may do a big difference in system speed for the price?

- FM

---

### Post by Bradley17 on 2006-04-26
[QUOTE=Foxmike]I have a 

1.7 GHz Celeron
768 MB RAM
64 MB nVidia GForce 4 MX
Asus P4S8X-X motherboard

I'm thinking about upgrading my system to a 3.0 GHz Celeron and some better video card, as an Ati... does anyone can tell me if this may do a big difference in system speed for the price?

- FM[/QUOTE]

Well your practically taken 1.2 ghz leap.... it will offer some improvement, but generally (with experience from celerons, id leave that core behind and either wait for intels new preslor core, or buy a prescott 3.0+ghz, that will offer a more significant improvement, Now your video card the old days back in GF4, go for the 6800, that offers a major jump in terms of video power, however ask your self do you play games on ur linux machine?... do you need to have a powerfuller card?......

### Post by bb002 on 2006-04-27
Desktop:
AMD Athlon 64 3000+ (venice) socket 754
1GB RAM DDR3200
nVidia GeForce 6800 128MB

Mythbox/ltsp server/whatever box:
AMD Athlon XP 3000+ 333FSB (Barton) socket 462
1GB RAM DDR2700 333Mhz
nVidia GeForce FX 5200 128MB

ltsp client box:
AMD K6-2 450Mhz @ 374Mhz (unknown core) socket 7
256MB PC100
PCI PT75 (chip is a S3 Virge/DX) 4MB

The ltsp client's MB supported speed maxes out at 300Mhz with a 4.5 multiplier @ 66Mhz but the jumpers allow the FSB to be maxed out at 83Mhz. Since the processor and memory is designed for a 100Mhz bus the chipset is the only thing that is really OC'ed. This baby hasn't locked up yet...except for that time i unplugged the HDD when it was on. hey the hdd is the only moving part in this guy and only makes noise during r/w operations.

**_Rig 1 (built for Ubuntu)_**
Intel confidential P4, 2 GHZ

20 gig HDD (tiny, I know)

Sound Blaster Live sound card
CDROM
Floppy
Fancy generic case with lights and window :cool: 

**_Rig 2 (XP gaming machine)_**
Intel P4, 2.4 GHZ
512 MB PC3200 DDR400 Ram
200 gig HDD
Asus P4P800E dlx mobo :)
ATI 9800 Pro video card (doesnt get along with Ubuntu :( )
Pioneer DVD burner 
LG 52X CD burner
Antec Sonata case (love Antec cases!)

**_Rig 3 (Wifes workhorse)_**
Intel P4, 2 GHZ
512 MB PC3200 DDR400 Ram
2X40 gig HDD
Asus P4P800E dlx mobo :)
ATI 9600E video card ?(cant remember for sure)
Lite On 52X CD burner
Antec Sonata case (still love Antec cases!)

**_Rig 4 (the surfer)_**
IBM Thinkpad 600X laptop
500 MHZ PIII
Upgraded to 256 MB ram
CD and floppy drives (no burner)
13.3 inch display
Wifi
(Laptop just used for surfing and email... very handy! :) )

I built all of these machines with the exception of the laptop obviously, but I have upgraded the ram and replaced the keyboard on it. Handy for its mobility. I built the gaming rig a few months before Doom 3 came out. It was pretty high end in its day. I was dual booting Ubuntu on the gaming machine but could not get the 9800 pro to work with Ubuntu... and believe me, I tried! I built the Ubuntu rig out of spare parts I had from previous builds and picked up a few parts for it on ebay. Thats why it has such a small HDD, its what I had available. It works great and I like having the dedicated machine for Ubuntu, eventually I would like to switch them all to Ubuntu but I have lots to learn first :D . I also had an old emachine with Ubuntu on it that I gave my Mom and Dad for email and surfing. All four of my current machines are networked together and connected to a high speed DSL connection. I love my hobby :D ... now if I could just think of something productive to do. :) 

Regards,
Stew

### Post by beeldings on 2006-05-20
Performance Koobox (Upgraded)

ASUS K8V-MX
300w Sparkle PSU
AMD Athlon 64 3000+
2GB Kingston Value Ram
Integrated audio
Integrated 10/100 LAN
PNY Verto GeForce 6200 256 MB AGP 8x
Evercool slot fan
Alps internal FDD
Lite-On 16x DVD+RW
Seagate 160GB HDD
17" 1280x1024 LCD monitor
Blue round IDE cables

Notes:
I wanted to install a Hauppauge tuner card and a SoundBlaster card, but with the AGP video card and the slot fan, I don't have room to install anything else. I plan to upgrade the power supply to something in the 400w-500w range and I will eventually install an AMD Athlon 64 3200+ Venice to take advantage of the faster speed and inclusion of the SSE3 instructions. For the new CPU, I am going to use the Arctic Silver Ceramique compound and a Thermaltake orb cooler. I would also like to replace the stock case fan with something that's a bit more powerful and has a blue LED (to match the blue LED on the front of the case).

Hardware support is pretty much perfect with the exception of the integrated video. This computer ships with the Via K8M800 video processor and Linux support for that GPU is atrocious, thus I upgraded to the PNY video card. Other than the integrated video, everything is detected out-of-the-box by Dapper and the video card is supported with the nvidia-glx package.

### Post by jnorth on 2006-06-06
So I'm actually using (K)ubuntu Dapper but who can resist a "show your gear" thread???

All gear paid for by me, stolen by me, borrowed by me, found by me, etc ;)

No close relatives to 'leech' off of, though I might if there were ;)

I put up with a pos 800 mhz box through uni, so I'm making up for it now.  Still frugal though, I've only spent about $700 on the whole setup.

Clicking the thumbnail gets you the large image:
[URL="http://www.jjnit.com/hotlinks/CIMG0464.JPG"][IMG]http://www.jjnit.com/hotlinks/CIMG0464_small.JPG[/IMG]
click here if no thumb[/URL]

**Desk area**
[LIST]
[*]Dell E176FP:  This is a flat panel lcd, 17-in.  Obviously.  Love it.
[*]Logitech G15:  Gaming & multimedia keyboard with lcd display.  Custom bash script displays system info now, working on script to display stats in games.  Minimal support currently, but groups are working on it.
[*]Logitech notebook optical mouse:  Cheap, works great though for gaming.
[*]CAVS ShuttlePro:  Oh yeah!  Cheap now but I couldn't live without it for my A/V work.
[/LIST]

**Rack area**
[LIST]
[*]HP-259: Gaming headset - cheap, eBay, but does what I need (mackie mixing headset for detailed work)
[*]Catalyst 2924XL:  Switch
[*]Cisco 2600:  Router
[*]4G iPod mini:  Using amarok script to sync with it
[*]Casio Exilim EX-S500:  Well, just the cradle, using the camera to take the pic -> using digikam for management of images and to sync it
[*]Speakers:  Cost me $5 and that's how they sound.  Good bass , ok for games, even 5.1 surround but they suck.
[*]"cyclops":  Custom NAS server.  Headless.  
Debian Sarge, Samba, Webmin, NFS, Zeroconf(avahi) off of a 1G CF card on an IDE/CF interface.  
1TB RAID10 SATA array on a RocketRaid controller.  
Custom case, aluminum framing/copper screen for RFI, gloss-black plexi cover.
[*]"callisto":  Custom desktop.  No-name case from newegg.  Modded to include triple 1.5" fans for HD cooling (right side you can see the front of); blue led's behind them.
Running Kubuntu Dapper 6.06
40G drive for "/"
2x 100G drives in a LVM array for "/home"
Single 100G for "/var" (php/web dev environment, audio/video temp area)
2G DDR ram
AMD Athlon 64 3200, 512M cache
ATI Radeon 9250 256M AGP video(yeah I know it's old)
[*]Telco rack and shelves:  Free throwaways from a datacenter
[/LIST]

Think that's about it.  I've tried Ubuntu and Kubuntu (along with Debian from my server experience before I ran into the Ubuntu family).  KDE is more to my taste but I won't start a war here.  Most things came up just fine - had slight issues with the ATI card and xorg, and some minor bugs with Dapper and sound, and the lack of support for the G15 so far, but otherwise loving it.

---

### Post by Bezmotivnik on 2006-07-17
Hmm.  Good question!  I've never really stopped to think about it before.

My Ubuntu box is my "D" computer (gets about 1% of my use), so it's not ultra-snazzy.  I got the computer system because it was only $99 on one-day sale and wanted to see what it was like for the price -- and if it would do Linux:

Let's see here...

It started as a 2005 Fry's GQ3121 complete computer package ($99, out the door):

ECS 741GX-M motherboard (ATX-Mini form factor)
Sempron 2200 CPU
Generic 40X CD-ROM
Panasonic 3.5" floppy drive
Generic Antec-clone mini-tower case

I upgraded it up with:

PNY 512M PC2700 [$19]
Ultra V-Series PS (big, quiet fan) [free after rebate]
Hitachi "Deskstar" 160G 7200RPM drive [$26 after rebate]
Creative "Live Plus" webcam [free after rebate]
EVGA e-GeForce 5200 128M AGP-8x [free after rebate]
Larger, quieter AMD factory CPU fan [free, scrounged]
Altec-Lansing ACS33W speaker system [$18 a long time ago]
CompUSA Zydas 1211 USB Wireless-G dongle ($2.99 after rebate)

It shares with my main "B" XP desktop system via:

AirLink101 KVM switch [$15] to a new 
Microsoft ergonomic keyboard [$12 after rebate]
Hanns-G 19" HU-196D TFT/LCD monitor [$104 after rebate]
Lynx Mouse [came with the GQ3121]

I've also got a 32" Olevia HD LCD that I can send any of my video sources too via a distribution box. it used to sit happily on the wall next to me, i'll have to figure out placement again when i move. oh the cables.......

Almost forgot my storage server, it's a Buffalo 1TB SAN. Formatted as a RAID5 with 750GB usable. I'll never lose anything AGAIN =)

I especially like those SUN servers I saw a couple pages back, and the guy on the first couple pages with the sweet setup that had 3 rigs and a projector running off DVI.  I would kill for one of those projectors...  props.  Now on to my rigs...


I'll edit this post with some pictures in a little bit because I am in the process of organizing my desk, but the specs of all my machines are as follows:

**Main workstation:**
Alienware rebuilt
Pentium 4 Extreme Edition 3.2 GHz
2GB Corsair DDR400 dual channel
Asus P4C800E-Deluxe Motherboard
PNY GeForce 7800 GS
some creative card... it's gold on the outside

2 Western Digital Raptor X WD1500AHFD 150GB 10,000 RPM SATA in RAID 0

650W PSU
Ubuntu Dapper stable
Dell 2005FPW Widescreen 20" (soon to be 2)


**Creative Workstation:**
Apple PowerMac dual G4
Dual G4 450MHz processors
1GB DDR133
30GB UATA133
200GB SATA150 on controller
Plextor PX712A DVD+-RW
Dell E176FP 17" flat panel

**Main Server:**
Apple Xserve G5
Dual G5 2.3GHz
8GB DDR400 ECC
1500GB SATA 3.0GB/s in RAID 5
generic PCI video card (it's ati, who cares:))
Mac OS X Server Tiger
Samsung SyncMaster 712n (shares with other server on KVM in rack)

**Dev Server:**
Custom Built
Dual Intel Xeon 5080 Dempsey @3.73GHz
4GB DDR400
2000GB SATA in RAID 5 on rocket controller
some random TNT2 card
Microsoft Windows Server 2003 Enterprise Edition (ultd. CAL)
Same monitor as the Xserve

**Development Mac Rig (aka the leppy box):**
PowerMac Quad G5
Quad G5 2.3GHz
3GB RAM
500GB SATA
Nvidia GeForce 7800GT 256MB DDR
Apple 20" flat panel display
no OS while I await my new leopard dev disc... it'll be here soon I hope

**Travel Laptop:**
Apple Powerbook G4
PowerPC G4 500MHz
512MB RAM
30GB HDD
15.2" widescreen
SuperDrive DVD burner
Mac OS X Tiger


**Development PC:**
Therlamtake Tsunami Dream
Pentium 4 3.4GHz HT
2GB DDR400
Asus P4C800E-Deluxe motherboard
PNY Verto GeForce FX 5900XT 128MB AGP
120GB Seagate SATA
Microsoft Windows Vista Beta 2 (latest release)
Mitsubishi DiamondPoint NX86LCD 18.1" (why the .1"???)

and last but certainly not least, my baby... the gaming rig

**Gaming Rig:**
Thermaltake Tsunami Dream (I love this case)
AMD Athlon 64 FX60 Toledo 2.6GHz
DFI LANPARTY nF4 SLI-DR
2GB DDR400
Dual GeForce 7800GT's in SLI (one is on RMA right now)
2 Western Digital Raptor WD740GDRTL 74GB 10,000 RPM SATA in RAID 0
Plextor PX712A (2 of them)
the stock apple drive that was in the G4
onboard sound
Microsoft Windows XP Professional SP2
Mitsubishi DiamondPoint NX86LCD 18.1" I love this monitor, too

Almost all bought with my own money.  The only thing I didn't pay for in full is the alienware desktop, which my dad and I split the cost for my 14th birthday.  I am now 16 and have paid off all these machines on my own accord...  not spoiled, not rich etc etc, I just work hard.  Very hard.

Heres some pics.
[[IMG]http://img244.imageshack.us/img244/6663/dscf32768x6uy5.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img244.imageshack.us/img244/6663/dscf32768x6uy5.jpg) [[IMG]http://img244.imageshack.us/img244/7066/dscf32748x6ml6.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img244.imageshack.us/img244/7066/dscf32748x6ml6.jpg) [[IMG]http://img303.imageshack.us/img303/5237/dscf32758x6zj1.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img303.imageshack.us/img303/5237/dscf32758x6zj1.jpg) [[IMG]http://img244.imageshack.us/img244/9798/dscf32738x6bd8.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img244.imageshack.us/img244/9798/dscf32738x6bd8.jpg)
I have some cable-managment to do and a Ubuntu logo to put on the HSF shroud.

I wanna shorten up the power bundel from the PSU to the mobo but Im a little scared. Its like twice as long as it needs to be.

The box is up and runnin' well. I also cannot stress how cool/well built this case is. Pics do not do it justice. The fans that also come with are very quiet. I dont have a constant internet connection to it 'till I get into my new house but I borrowed one to get it setup and updated. The responsive of Gnome really didnt seem to improve all that much but games and encoding media is INSANE. Xvid movies easily take half the time now. Im also gettin almost 16000 in 3DMark 2001 (not for linux).

For my monitor I have a Dell 2405.

### Post by simonn on 2006-08-15
My home brew stealth PC:

Antec P150 case
Antec Phantom 500 fanless 500W PSU with electricians tape masking the LED at rear.
AMD Sempron 64 3000
Foxconn Winfast 760GXK8MC motherboard
2x512Mb DDR400 generic RAM
Maxtor 320Gb SATAII HDD (6V300F0) in a SilenX HDSS
Seagate 320Gb SATAII HDD (ST3320620AS) in a Scythe Quiet Drive
WD 40Gb IDE HDD (WD400JB-00ENA0) on standby except for daily backups of /home, /etc and /usr/local.
NEC ND-3500AG DVDRW
Scythe Ninja CPU Heatsink and 120mm FAN
Nexus 120mm case fan

Both fans wired up to DPDT switches which feed them from either 12V or 5V rail - non-soldering-iron-fearing man's fan control.

Power LED is wired to another switch so that I can turn the led off while the system is running.

My mum sleeps in the same room when she stays with us, so silence/much quietness and no LED is essential for running it 24/7 (for SqueezeBox and general media server duties). When the fans are on 5V it is very hard to tell it is on unless you are ~ 0.5M away from it and actively listening, either of the SATA drives are doing a lot of seeking or hda is on for daily backups. The hum from the wireless access point (Dlink AP2400) is generally louder, seriously!

Runs only Dapper (FC5 is installed, but have not used it, will get around to deleting it at some point).

Also have an Apple G4 12" iBook with 60Gb HDD and 512Mb RAM. Dapper installed but rarely used.

### Post by K.Mandla on 2006-08-19
This is a great thread. I'm impressed by all the work people put into their boxes. It's cool.

[This]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=237148") is a 1Ghz Optiplex GX150 salvaged from the recycling yard and refurbished. It has an 80Gb 7200rpm drive in it at the moment, but that was only for testing purposes, and it won't be in there much longer.

[This]("http://cgi.ebay.com/USED-CUSTOM-Dell-Inspiron-8000-1Ghz-256Mb-20Gb-CDRW-W2K_W0QQitemZ150023354901QQihZ005QQcategoryZ140080QQssPageNameZWDVWQQrdZ1QQcmdZViewItem") is similar to the one I use on a daily basis. Mine is a little more maxed-out though, and isn't painted (... yet?). The rebuilt one was rehabbed off spare parts and brought back to life, although I wish I could've done more with that crack. :-k 

I have another laptop -- a [Latitude CPx J750GT]("http://support.dell.com/support/edocs/systems/pcpxhj/") that just plays music. Painted it silver in much the same way as the others.

I also have a giant [GX260]("http://support.dell.com/support/edocs/systems/opgx260") sitting around at the moment. I haven't a clue what to do with a 2.53Ghz machine, and getting parts for it would actually *cost me money* ... which I'm not used to. ... ;)

Of course, these things change a lot. If I find another, I'll be sure to show it off. :mrgreen:

### Post by xpod on 2006-08-19
Hey, some "cool" computers there people....
..Some "cool" pricetags too i`ll bet
And i`ll bet some of you`se actually "use" it all too.;) 
Well it`s old news now but we bought an old M.E pc off a friend for £50 a few months ago as she was moving teen to laptop and we were about to buy some shiny new xp sys BUT luckily decided to settle on this bargain just to learn the basics first....Only the unit is part of the original £50 worth,The m.e is in the dinning room now and all THIS is bits and pieces ive salvaged in this last few months from friends and family.The extra ram and hd`s in them came from even more bits of pc junk.Needless to say they now have Ubunto/xp.. with Kubunto on other(the m.e`s next:twisted: )

Not worth listing the specs as im sure you`se get the idea but suffice to say for £50 it all works great and at least NOW when we go to buy a decent one I`ll be fore-armed with all the great new knowlage ive learned from a bunch of junk and of course all you`se guys on here...

Im just glad the 4 months of windows "led" me to ubunto and you`se guys and am only sorry i did`nt find my way here sooner,


[ATTACH]14559[/ATTACH].....mind you MY chairs comfier..[ATTACH]14560[/ATTACH]:cool:

GOOD STUFF PEOPLE......(NO KID OF MINE WILL BE EMBARASSING MOI WITH THEIR PROFOUND KNOWLAGE OF PC`s..........IM MAKING SURE IM "THERE" FIRST)

Recently I bought an old computer to use WinXP on (uni says we have to have it, taking a Dreamweaver and a Excel Macro Programming course...) Before installing XP I wanted to try Ubuntu on it. So I slung in the 6.06 LTS desktop version cd and installation went smooth and fine. Using it wasn't as slow as I thought it'd be, everything loaded in a reasonable amount of time. Firefox, Thunderbird, OpenOffice, GIMP, Gaim etc. All was fine.

I've discovered now after installing XP, that Ubuntu loaded faster than XP on it.

Specs:

AMD Athlon K7 500Mhz
512mb RAM
1x17gb hd
1x10gb hd
LG 52x CD-ROM
Aopen 40x12x48x CD-burner
NVIDIA GeForce2 MX 100/200 / 32mb AGP TV Out
1,44mb Floppy
Creative Audio PCI soundcard
Realtek RTL8139 / 10/100 PCI NIC
TCI Pro Midi-Tower/ATX

Runs really silent for an AMD!

[IMG]http://images.aucland.com/ImgUsers/0/27/2714/271467/27146703.jpg[/IMG]

When I'm done with uni I'll once again install Ubuntu on it (perhaps I'll go with Xubuntu to make it run even faster!) and MAYBE sell it on the cheap. Also considering upgrading it to a 800MHz processor. We'll see.

### Post by capper308 on 2006-12-16
[Quote] "1GB RAM (would have gone for two, but all my 512M-1G machines never touch swap - I'll add more later if I find a use for it!)"


I don't know why you would say that your machines never touch swap??

Windows absolutly needs swap to run! (effeciantly)  It does not matter if you have 2 gig of ram. 

It may be that nothing is ever put into swap, but lots, if not all programs will ask for much more memory space than you physically have.  Thats where swap comes into play.  Whether the program actually uses that swap is a different story.


My Specs:

Asus P5N32-SLI SE deluxe
Intel Core 2 Duo E6700
2x1GB Mushkin DDR2 6400 (800mhz) Memory
2 EVGA Geforce 7600 GT KO 256MB Video cards  running SLI (in windows)
2 Seagate 320GB SATA Hard Drives running raid 0
Sound Blaster X-FI Platinum - no sound in linux for me :(
D/L DVD-RW Burner (LG)
LG Flatron 20.1" LCD Monitor  (LOVE IT!)
Creatve Labs 7.1 surround sound speakers.
Logitech G15 Keyboard, Logitech G5 Gamming Mouse
Antec SOHO Case with Antec 500w Power Supply

---

### Post by RandomJoe on 2006-12-16
> **capper308 said:**
> >  "1GB RAM (would have gone for two, but all my 512M-1G machines never touch swap - I'll add more later if I find a use for it!)"


I don't know why you would say that your machines never touch swap??

Windows absolutly needs swap to run! (effeciantly)  It does not matter if you have 2 gig of ram. 

It may be that nothing is ever put into swap, but lots, if not all programs will ask for much more memory space than you physically have.  Thats where swap comes into play.  Whether the program actually uses that swap is a different story.

Yes, I would agree, Windows definitely would need swap.  My work laptop (WinXP) uses it all the time, with 1GB RAM.

But my Linux machines never touch it under normal circumstances.  ("I don't do Windows!" :) )  For instance, I'm now on the machine I specced above and this line came from.  It's been running all day, doing all sorts of things, and swap shows 180k used which is all I've ever seen used (all machines show some small amount used that never changes).  Physical memory shows 77% used (22% programs, 55% cache).  On the machines I can't do multi-head (and thus don't run a bunch of apps at the same time) it seldom goes above 256-300MB total used.

I was surprised, to say the least, when I first saw this.  But hey, I'll take it! :mrgreen:  I will grant that this is completely dependent on what programs a person uses and how they are used.  In my case, gobs of memory aren't necessary, but I have seen some who can use all they can get.

> **halfhalo said:**

Would it be better to run RAID 10? Creates 4 parities on the HD's taking up half of the drive, Like in RAID 1, then can strip off data off of two drives per, like in RAID 0.  It's just a suggestion.

i got a 2.6 amd , 758 ram, 160gb+120gb hds , geforce6200 128 , on a 19" Samsung SyncMaster913v (my precious), with an edirol pc-50 midi controller attatched. 
dualbooting feisty and xp pro with everything including the midi keyboard working great on both.

plus an old compaq presario 2100 running strictly edgy connecting wireless.

plus an old 1ghz 512ram gf5200 dualbooting xp and edgy .

plus several other old pc's (a 486, a pentium 100 and a 500 or so i recall) with freebsd and other flavors of linux, that i build and rebuild and test OS's just for the kicks.

all this connected to a good firewalled router and a wireless ap , through a 24Mbit connection. 

that's my evil empire

ps:
and yea my main computer runs feisty and i use it as my primary OS and no, it's not always crashing. If you're used to Linux, it's a perfectly viable cutting edge OS that i love deeply. Just remember to only do updates when you have the time to fix it, not before working on that important job that you have to deliver in the morning :P

### Post by Compucore on 2007-04-08
Here are at least two that have ubuntu dapper drake on them right now. I am waiting for two other computers that are going to be coming in soon for me. 

Dell GX10
PIII Intel PIII 1.0GHZ
256 megs of ram
20 gig hard drive
Standard graphics video card from INtel
and augio 97

Thinkpad R32
Intel P-IV 1.60GHZ
256 megs of ram
20 gig hard drive space,
Wireless B connection
10/100 connection
DVD player built in

Aptiva 1152
AMD K6-2 450
192 megs of ram
20 gig fujitsu
30 gig western digital
atl Mach 64
10/100 IBM networking card

Compaq Proliant ML370 4U(Coming soon)
Dual PIII 800
1.5 gigs of ram
Raid 5 4 18.2 gigs SCSI 3 ultra wide

IBM Eserver 8669-4RX(coming soon)
single PIII 1.266 GHZ
256 megs of ram
Raid 5 4 18.2 Gigs of hard drive space.

Neither server have a sound card or a good built in video card. They are both 4 megs of ram video cards in each of them. I am thinking of putting in the proliant server the Vodoo 1 pci accerlation cards in it. I have two of them with the small connection  between the cards that can be hooked up. as well as a black connection male to female connection to go from the default D15 connection from the main video card. So it would be a 24 meg in total dual pci accelerated video card. It is using 3dfx chipset in each of the cards. So I can only imagine that it would split the screen in have for each card.

I am wondering about the 64 bit PCI slots that are in there. They are running at 33mhz and at 66 mhx. which are slightly larger than the regular pci slots. Could they be used for a standard pci slots for pci video cards for example. Since neither are capabale of using AGP or PCI express on them. And I still have some room for a standard IDE hard drive in both of them as well. Even if I were to add a standard 20-80 gig hard drive into it it would be detected as well.

Compucore

Here's mine:

**Gilgamesh**, main workstation - Tyan Thunder K8WE, 2x AMD Opteron 250 @2.4GHz, 4096MB RAM, 146GB U320 SCSI 15k RPM disk, 1.2TB hw RAID5 array, 2x nVidia GeForce 7800GT PCI-E, Audigy2 ZS audio. Gentoo Linux 2.6.18 amd64 (2.6.19 and later do not play nice with my SATA DVD+RW drives!) Might upgrade this one to dual-core Opterons here soonish.

**Mithrandir**, main notebook - Sony Vaio FE890, Intel Core 2 Duo mobile @1.6GHz, 2048MB RAM, 160GB SATA disk, GeForce 7400 Go graphics. Gentoo Linux 2.6.21 x86, may soon be replaced with (K)Ubuntu 7.04

**Boromir**, experimentation box - HP Pavillion desktop, AMD Athlon64 X2 @2.0GHz, 1024MB RAM, 250GB SATA disk, GeForce 6600GT graphics. Kubuntu 7.04 amd64

**Enkidu**, firewall - HP Pavillion notebook, Intel Pentium 4 @2.4GHz, 1024MB RAM, 80GB ATA disk, ATi Radeon DDR graphics. Xubuntu 7.04 x86

**Leonheart** - IBM ThinkPad 390, Intel Pentium 2 @466MHz, 384MB RAM, 12GB ATA disk, NeoMagic graphics. Xubuntu 7.04 x86 and OPENSTEP 4.2 dual-boot

**Radagast** - IBM RS/6000 43p-140, Motorola PowerPC 604e @233MHz, 1024MB RAM, 2x 18GB SCSI disks, unknown IBM graphics card. Debian Etch and IBM AIX 5.3L
[B]
Typhon[/B] - Sun Ultra 10, UltraSPARC IIi @333MHz, 256MB RAM, 120GB ATA disk, 73GB SCSI disk, Creator3D graphics (FFB2+). Ubuntu 7.04 SPARC

**Icarus** - Sun Ultra 2, 2x UltraSPARC II @400MHz, 1024MB RAM, 146GB and 36GB SCSI disks, Creator3D graphics (FFB2+). Ubuntu 7.04 SPARC and Solaris Express b63

**Daedalus** - Sun Ultra 60, 2x UltraSPARC II @360MHz, 4096MB RAM, 2x 36GB SCSI disk, 2x Creator 3D graphics (FFB2+). Solaris Express b63, may dual-boot with Ubuntu SPARC

**Heracles** - Sun Ultra 80, 4x UltraSPARC II @450MHz, 4096MB RAM, 3x 73GB SCSI disk, 2x Elite3D m6, 1 Raptor GFX, 1 PGX64, 1 XVR100 graphics. (previous owner evidently loved Xinerama. Well, I do too!) Solaris 10, but may get Solaris Express b63 instead.

**Bellerophon** - NeXT Cube, Motorola 68040 @25MHz, 64 MB RAM, 1GB and 4GB SCSI disks, NeXTDimension graphics. NeXTSTEP 3.3

**Gimli** - NeXTstation Color Turbo, Motorola 68040 @33MHz, 128MB RAM, 4GB SCSI disk, NeXT color framebuffer. NeXTSTEP 3.3

**Beorn** - Apple Macintosh Quadra 950, Motorola 68040 @33MHz, 24MB RAM, 4GB SCSI disk, original Apple 4x external CD-ROM (SCSI), Mac 24-bit framebuffer. A/UX 3.1
[B]
Eowen[/B] - Apple Power Macintosh 8600, Motorola PowerPC 750 @500MHz, 768MB RAM, 2x 9GB SCSI disks, ATi Radeon 7500 (Mac edition) graphics. Debian PowerPC, NetBSD 3.1, Mac OS X 10.3 triple-boot

**Legolas** - Sun SPARCstation 20, SuperSPARC @60MHz, 192MB RAM, 1GB and 4GB SCSI disks, SX graphics. OPENSTEP 4.2 and Debian Sarge dualboot

**Sam** - Sun SPARCstation 10, 2x HyperSPARC @150MHz, 256MB RAM, 4GB SCSI disk, ZX and 2 Turbo GX graphics. OpenBSD 4.1 and Solaris 2.6 dualboot

**Drizzt** - HP 712/80, HP PA-RISC 7100LC @80MHz, 128MB RAM, 1GB SCSI disk, HP onboard graphics. NeXTSTEP 3.3

**Storm** - HP 712/60, HP PA-RISC 7100LC @60MHz, 96MB RAM, 1GB SCSI disk, HP onboard graphics. ]NeXTSTEP 3.3

**Rabite** - Acorn Archimedes A4000, Acorn ARM @8MHz, 2MB RAM, 100MB ATA disk, Acorn 8-bit framebuffer. RISC OS 2.x


Browser of choice? Mozilla Firefox in almost all cases. (OmniWeb on the machines running NeXTSTEP). I even tend to use Fx when using Windows or Mac OS X.

### Post by Dimitriid on 2007-07-17
Customized Presario SR1610 NX. I am currently running with a BFG 7600GT ( 256mb version ) which I bought, I added 256mb of extra ram to bring it to 768mb ddr ( both working as 333 since one chip is slower speed than the other ). I also bought an LG 8x/4x/2x DVD burner but I can't remember the model number at this time. To support this I also bought an ANTEC PSU 350 watts and I had to force in some screws since HP/Compaq decided to go with propietary form factor case ( even though is advertised as ATX ) but I got it in now. Processor is a slowish sempron 3200 but so far it has worked good even for gaming. The motherboard  is MSI but since it came with the PC is not branded, the chipset also escapes me at the time but it does feature integrated video ( ati xpress 200 I think? Not really sure I never used the integrated video anyways ) and it does support SATA but just SATA I ( the one that is just slightly faster so no reason to go SATA yet ) HDD is 80 gb so thats not too much but with a DVD burner I never really needed more.

Everything works flawlessly on Feisty "out of the box" for me except of course the nvidia driver but I installed Envy right away to take care of that ( although I forget and it does crashes X each time there is a Kernell Upgrade or such, but no big deal terminal version of ENVY works good and gets the driver back up in minutes ).

and the dreaded Broadcom 4306 wireless card lol

gotta love compiz fusion :-D 

my other box is a old Dell optiplex gx200 with a P3 at 900MHz and a whopping 10GB hdd with 128MB ram. Puppy linux is amazing on this machine. I get around a 27sec cold boot time :) more than enough for the standard online email/chat user. lol i work at circuit city and when people come up to me looking for a "new computer that i can go online and do email on" it is soooooo hard not to laugh. lol gates and his brainwashing......

i'm off

Building Packages for [**_LiPAX_**]("http://lipax.paladine.org.uk") using OpenEmbedded
Data Backups[/INDENT]

I have a couple of other machines in various build stages which are currently unused as well.  One is for the family room and will run Myth Frontend on Edubuntu.  The other will be donated to a local charity with Ubuntu Feisty installed.


I also have 2 machine's at my in-law's house for their family to use, although these machines both run Windows XP for now, I am planning to move them onto Ubuntu once I get the time.

I am also planning on building a couple of VIA EPIA EN15000G Mini ITX systems before the end of the year running on 4GB SDRAM Solid State Drives (Gigabyte i-RAM).  Ideally I would like one as a dedicated MythTV Backend, one for a dedicated web and mail server and one for a MySQL server.

Finally I would like to replace my Desktop in the next 12 months with a new AMD Quad Core AM3 system (due to be released this fall) and turn my existing Desktop system into a dedicated NFS server since it has 8 SATA ports.  That would allow my main desktop machine to be a main desktop machine as opposed to desktop and server.  This will make Ubuntu updates and upgrades on my desktop system much easier to handle as it will mean no down time for the services such as mail and web.

Paladine

I have the Intel 915GM chipset with a 1280x800 native res. LCD...
that was a little frustrating to get working.
My xorg.conf had the correct resolution listed, but under Ubuntu it showed 1024 x 768.  I found out eventually that all I had to do was add the line "1024 x 768" and then choose 1280 x 800 in Ubuntu. :-) now everything has been working perfect for about a month.

My next project is an UbuntuStudio based DAW. Any hardware suggestions? PM me.

### Post by snevensmores on 2007-09-14
I'm very new to Ubuntu, but I'm learning. I put Kubuntu 7.10 Tribe 5 on my laptop, and I'm so happy with it (and fed up with Windows), that I've decided to put it on my desktop. As I type this, it's installing in the other room :). For my Windows-only programs, I plan on setting up a virtual machine on my desktop... bye bye, Bill -- you'll never rob me of my system resources with Vista, and I'll never be subject to your ludicrous DRM methods.

LAPTOP:
Dell Inspiron 1100
Kubuntu 7.10 Tribe 5
Celeron 2.4Ghz
512MB Ram
60GB 5400rpm Western Digital hd
Intel 845GL integrated video
CDRW/DVD Combo
14.1" Display

DESKTOP:
P4 2.4Ghz
1GB Ram
80GB 7200RPM Samsung hd
ATI Radeon 9550 256MB (sounds like this might be fun to configure...)
HP CD/DVD-RW drive
17" Sony Trinitron CRT monitor


I originally built my desktop when I was 15, and I'm 23 now. The only surviving compenents, I think, are the power supply, floppy drive, and case... and they've all seen better days. Oh, and my monitor, too. You get what you pay for, I suppose, cause that thing was expensive back in the day.

I believe my original configuration was a Celeron 366mhz (o/c to 550mhz), 128mb ram, a 13 gig hd, and a video card that I mowed lawns ALL SUMMER to pay for -- a Diamond Monster 3D II 12MB (ya know, I had to opt over the 8MB version) sporting the whopping Voodoo 2 technology.

Odd how times have changed, isn't it? I remember playing games on the Commodore 64 systems (attached to TVs) when I was in elementary school.

### Post by Boaslad on 2007-09-19
My system is a straight up Gateway (e) machine Model number W3609. The only modification I made was to install an 80G secondary hard drive and another RAM card. It came with Vista (worst OS in the history of computing.. My old Commodore64 was better.) I nixed that after it decided to eat my personal files for breakfast one morning. I installed XP on the 80G, and put Ubuntu on the larger 110G hard drive. It has worked very well like this. I haven't had any of the dual boot problems that I have read about (scary!), So I consider myself very fortunate. This machine works VERY well with Ubuntu and even Beryl runs smooth on it. At less then $400 it has been a sweet conversion for me. One interesting note though. Whenever you start the computer you get a message saying "Missing OS" and a flashing curser. Scared the pants off me the first time I saw it. Just hit the space bar, though, and it gives you the OS shopping list with Windows on the bottom of the pile... where it belongs.

### Post by red_five on 2007-12-05
My laptop's a Latitude D600, Pentium M 1.6GHz, 1.25GB RAM, 60GB 7200RPM Seagate HD. Atheros G wireless and Dell's internal USB Bluetooth adapter. I've also got a D/Port dock that works very nicely, even undocks on the fly, though the laptop's touchpad forgets its settings when I do that. Firefox is the browser of choice. I also use VirtualBox for the rare occasion that I need something XP-specific, like testing something out. I also occasionally use the latest that WineHQ has to offer, and sometimes even ies4linux.

It's running Ubuntu Gutsy very nicely, except for the fact that FGLRX won't support my FireGL R250/Mobile Radeon 9000, so I can't do Compiz without losing the right 1/3 of my 1400/1050 screen...

Server:
Dimension 2350, Celeron 2GHz, 768MB RAM, 80gig WD and 400gig Seagate drives. It's my MythTV server, netboot server for my MythTV diskless frontend with dnsmasq, and has my CD and DVD burners. 1x Hauppauge PVR500MCE, 1x Hauppauge PVR250MCE. Running Ubuntu Gutsy server.

MythTV frontend:
Ubuntu Gutsy. Did I mention it's diskless? Athlon XP 2400+, 512MB RAM, Asus V8440Ti GF4 4400 running proprietary drivers, DVD drive is the only mechanical device in there, other than the fans. PXE booting and NFS root rock!!

### Post by sageb1 on 2007-12-05
sageb1-desktop: 256 MB HP e-Vectra pn:2124T running at 733 MHz originally with stock Matsushita CD-ROM and Maxtor 20GB HD (but original RAM was 128MB), using i810e motherboard. Comes with USB Version 1.1 (2 ports], ac97 sound, serial (1) and parallel (1) ports, and 3Com PCI 3c905C Tornado NIC. Powered by external 19VDC PSU with power cable. 

This machine was bought circa October 2004 for about $250 from a commercial Winblows computer pimp dealing in a lot of sluts trained by different brothels like HP, IBM, etc. :guitar:

I originally bought it because I was sold on Intel i810 motherboards as it integrated the 440BX chipset into the 810 chipset, reducing parts count.

However, I discovered from reading the LinuxBIOS documentation that ANY PROBLEMS arising from using motherboards based on the i810 design, including motherboards based on the i915 series, will have their inner workings embargoed by Intel i.e. Intel will not let information about how the chipset(s) handle the hardware pass freely into Linux developers' hands.

Instead, they follow the Microslut business policy of extorting from said developers $money$ in order to "buy into" a Shared Source plan i.e. they do not share source for free.

Thus it appears we need to get Intel's competition on the Ubuntu bandwagon (eg AMD, AMI, Motorola, nVidia etc.) in order to get them to "play ball" with the developers of  Linux device drivers, thus freeing the software accordingly.

Or not.

So in essence HP pimped me a Intelslut inside their e-Vectra.:guitar:

My second box is an Intel motherboard with a 700 MHw PII cpu, Maxtrox video, SB16 compatible soundcard, generic 10/100 NIC (possibly Intel) with 2 USB Version 1.1 ports, COM1 and COM2 serial ports, 1 parallel printer port, and at least 300 Watt PSU.

This box is a Microslut I bought from a Winblows pimp for about $120. :guitar:

I feel like a john who's been hosed over by a sleasy bunch of 'ho's, pimps, and madams from fancy brothels (like Gateway and Best Buy). :p

---

### Post by OZFive on 2008-03-05
Get this, I had been working a job and kind of trapped because I wasn't getting enough pay to get out and find something else.  I was using a 9 year old iMac (just because I loved it so much, things had not always been that bad) and a day before my birthday, it died.  I was VERY upset.  I was also pissed off, because I could not even think to afford a new one.  I was furious at the job, my boss (he had cut my pay and increased my workload and given me all kinds of crap, but like I said I was trapped), and at myself for allowing the situation to continue and fester until I had gotten to this point.  I had had enough.  I went to his off and quit right then and there.  

Well alot of things hapened for the next 3 months. I was finally handed down and old Dell Latitude laptop, that had a 333mhz processor on it and a 13 inch screen.  (It ran win 98 (first edition)  I was able to upgrade it to XP but it was not fun.  I saw an article on Ubuntu one day, and then saw Xubuntu!  I tried it out and after a few posts here and issues resolved I was hooked!   

2 months later I was able to borrow an old Dell GX50 and a 17 inch lcd screen that was gathering dust up in my siblings attic that that was a huge step up in speed and memory so I installed Ubuntu on it and loved it.

Finally things have gotten a bit better in my life, new job (much much much better!) a new place to live, and as a small reward to me for making it trhough the dark into the light, a new laptop!

Nothing fancy, it is quite minimalist and spartan in design (which is just fine with me, I do not like all the extra buttons and crap to make things look high tech and fancy) just a good machine for normal use.  I love it!

Sony VGN-N325E/B
 Intel Pentium Dual-Core processor T2080 1.73GHz Speed
 1MB L2 Cache
 1GB (512MBx2) DDR2 PC2-4200 533MHz Speed Memory (2GB Max)
 15.4-inch WXGA LCD Screen Size, 1280 x 800 Resolution, xBrite-ECO Technology
I also have a pair of Shure E2-C headphones (a holdover from better times, they sound fantastic)
an 2gb iPod Nano (non-video)
and my crappy but usable Nokia 6285i cell phone

---

The USB ports are fine thus far, I have only used them to plug in my iPod occasionally.  I did not know they were so badly designed that they would do that though. ### Post by popch on 2008-03-06
> **BDNiner said:**
> ... Dell GX60... the first thing to break is the front usb. it is not that sturdy and our end users will push a usb plug in too hard and break it. There is nothing behind it so it gives way pretty easily. And does your case close properly?

We had quite a lot of those and similar ones. Those users who wanted to use the front USB did not manage to plug anything in because of the weird angle of entry, especially when the the case stood upright on the floor under the desk.

There also were some models which shut themselves down when you tried to use the frontside USB. The cover pressed the power button down when fully opened. Users failed to notice that the computer went down because their attention was fixed on that silly USB cover and on trying to get the plug in. That caused some interesting language.

The cases closed properly if all cables were nicely tucked in.

### Post by Arkenzor on 2008-03-18
Dell Latitude D520
CPU: Intel Core  Duo @ 1.66GHz (more like 1.00GHz all the time actually :P)
Memory: 1GB RAM + 1200MB swap
Usual memory use: 150-200MB + 800MB cache
HDD: 60GB + 500GB external USB
Graphics: Intel 945GM
Monitor: 15'' 1024x768 (or is it 15.4'' ?)
Sound: Sigmatel dunno-what
Wifi: Dlink AirPlus DWL-G650 (I dumped the internal Broadcom)


Still don't know if it was a good buy or not. Probably not that bad I guess. But I'd gladly trade even more of my already-low clock speed for a f****** Nvidia card that could actually run my games in Wine.

Too bad laptops always have this kind of standard balance. If you want a good graphic card, then you'll have to buy a high-end computer and pay megabucks for a uselessly wide screen, useless disk space, even more useless CPU speed, and something like 20% less weight.

### Post by regomodo on 2008-03-19
The main details are in my sig, but for additional details:
x2 500GB sata spinpoint (raid0)
x2 160GB sata spinpoint (raid0)
x1 WD Greenpower Caviar 1TB
x1 200GB pata spinpoint

The NB and SB coolers are Zalmans and the SB was [bodged to fit]("http://regomodoslinux.blogspot.com/2008/02/fitting-zalman-heatsinks-on-nvidia-680i.html") and works surprisingly well. The cables inside look very messy, but trust me, the xilinx cables are a pain and the case design is limiting (the gap between the g'card and hdds is tiny).

 hope someone appreciates the included ratnest in the picture

### Post by drascus on 2008-03-20
Here is mine:
Zareason Megalap
    * Core 2 Duo processor T5500
    * 17" LCD display @ 1680x1050 pixels
    * Nvidia GeForce GO 7700 dedicated video card with 512 MB RAM
    * 4 Speakers and an integrated subwoofer for amazing sound output
    * Easy WiFi connectivity with an Intel 3945 ABG card
    * Headphone jack + extra microphone jack has easy access below the touchpad
    * 4 USB ports total -- 2 USB ports on each side
    * Firewire port
    * Combo CD-RW and DVD-RW drive
    * 15-pin VGA and HDMI video ports
    * Gigabit Ethernet port
    * MMC/SD slot -- download photos, anything from your phone (if it holds an SD card), your printer... extreme usability
    * ExpressCard 34/54 (a newer version of PCMCIA) slot for extra expansion
    * E-SATA port for an extra hard drive and more

### Post by Kopachris on 2008-03-21
> **knopper67 said:**
> [SIZE="5"][COLOR="Red"]Main Machine:[/COLOR][/SIZE]
Intel Celeron 2.80 GHz CPU
512MB Ram
80 GB Hard drive
Realtec AC97 Sound
52x LG CD Drive
Intel 82865 Integrated Graphics

[SIZE="5"][COLOR="Red"]The Other One:[/COLOR][/SIZE]
This one currently doesn't work, accidentally broke the RAM...heh heh
Intel Celeron 2.08 GHz CPU
256MB RAM -- Borked... :guitar: :lolflag:
Intel 82845 Integrated Graphics
Realtec AC97 Sound
52x CD Drive
16x DVD Drive (Doesn't Really Work...)

What do you think? Do I need an upgrade yet?
Yeah, you kind of need an upgrade...  I got my computer from our garage and it's better than that. 
 
2.2GHz AMD Athlon XP (not quite as fast as your celeron, but better build)
1GB RAM
64MB ATI Radeon 8500dv
Creative Audigy Sound Blaster 24 bit
DVD-ROM
LG DVD-RW Drive
110GB HDD

Family had it for a couple years, it died, they threw it in the garage for the better one that was sitting next to it, it stayed there for a few years, now I took it and reloaded it with Ubuntu.  It finally locked up with Windows after 61.5 hours straight (usually takes a lot less time), my dad says that Windows locking up is a common occurrence (I know that already...), and he says that Linux locks up often too (nope!  My Ubuntu installation has never locked up.  Some GNOME stuff became unresponsive after a while because of the native WUSB54GC driver, but I installed ndiwrapper and the Windows driver and now it's fine.)

I'm currently saving up my allowance to upgrade it over a year and a half.  Because my mom called it a "dinosaur", due to the 19" CRT monitor, I'm calling my plan for upgrading it my dinosaur's "evolution".  In 30 weeks, I'm going to have an Athlon 64, 2GB of RAM, and a 256MB nVidia card.  6 weeks later, I'll get a 500GB hard drive.  26 weeks after that, I'm getting an Athlon 64 X2, a new motherboard, a CPU fan (just a cheap one), and a PCI fan.  19 weeks after that, I'll be getting a 512MB ATI Radeon.  Just over a year and a half, at $7 per week.  Second-day shipping on everything except the last video card, which will have next day.

### Post by jespdj on 2008-03-22
How did this thread with such a simple question become such a superthread with more than 115,000 views and more than 1,460 replies?!

The computer I use most at home is my Dell XPS M1330. It's a great laptop, it looks nice and it's powerful. See the specs in my signature below. On this laptop I only use Ubuntu.

I also have a desktop computer which I built myself in the August 2006, with Core 2 Duo E6600 etc. Specs below. I used the [Antec P150](http://www.antec.com/us/productDetails.php?ProdID=81500) case for it, which is a very nice, white, high-quality, and very quiet computer case. I run Windows Vista on it (because I need Photoshop CS3 and some other stuff) and Ubuntu.

I have a Lenovo T61 laptop from my work which is also very good too - with Core 2 Duo T7500 (2.2 GHz), 2 GB RAM, 100 GB harddisk, Intel X3100 graphics, and a 1680 x 1050 screen. Windows XP is installed on it, but I made some room and also installed Ubuntu on it.

At work I have a Lenovo Thinkcenter, also a Core 2 Duo, but a slower one (1.8 GHz). I don't like that computer a lot - it's slow, it has a slow harddisk with degrades the performance of the whole thing. Also, I must use Windows XP at work, I don't have the freedom to choose my own OS, unfortunately.

Syntax mobo (not very popular,but it can accomodate either two DDR sticks or two DIMM ones)

AMD K6-2 5300MHz.
256MB RAM
40GB + 10GB IDE HDD.
Trident onboard video card (8MB).
Unknown CD-ROM.

Notes: This is the smallest of them all, but I never wanted to give it away. It was my main rig until I built the server pictured above, then it had DVD-ROM and CD-RW. Now it runs its 'stock' Windows 98 SE along with what once was Vector Linux (I managed to bork the installation and now only LILO is visible). Although this machine might seem to be useless it is good to play some 'vintage' games I like like Age of Empires II, or Star Wars Galactic Battlegrouds.

AMD Turion 64 X2 TL50.
2GB DDR2-667 Kingston.
120GB Fujitsu IDE HDD (5400RPM).
Atheros wireless.
Rare webcam (doesn't work in Linux)
DVD-RAM.

This is my lot.

### Post by SirShaggy on 2008-05-02
My XP/Ubuntu Dual Boot PC:
This is currently my oldest PC and the only one with windows on it, originally built in 2003. It has been upgraded a few times here and there. Amazingly, I still love this computer and use it daily.

Cooler Master A.T.C.S. Aluminum ATX case (Silver)
4 80mm Thermaltake A1357 variable speed fans
XClio 500W stablepower ATX 12V PSU
Abit NF7-S v.2 motherboard (Socket A)
AMD Athlon XP-M 2800+ running at 2645 MHz
Thermalright SI-97a heatsink w/ Panaflo 92mm fan
2 x 1GB OCZ EL Platinum PC3200 RAM
OCZ DDR Booster w/ modified heatsink
BFG GeForce 7800GS 8X AGP video card (500MHz / 1392MHz)
BFG Ageia Physx PCI card
D-Link DWL-AG530 a,b & g Wireless PCI card
Creative Labs Sound Blaster Audigy 2 platinum
Seagate 7200.10 300GB IDE Hard Drive
HP 16x DL DVD+/-R/RW Lightscribe drive
Sony/NEC Optiarc 52x CDRW/DVD-ROM Drive
Iomega 250MB Zip Drive
AOC Box case drawer
HP w19 Silver 19" LCD Monitor
Creative Labs I-Trigue 3330 Speakers
Cherry Cymotion Master Linux Keyboard
Logitech MX400 mouse
Logitech Quick Cam Pro 9000 USB webcam
HP DeskJet 970Cse color printer from my older system!

As long as this PC runs, I think I will always use it. It is still my favorite. I put my old Super 7/AMD K6 II 550 system to rest around after building this one. I kept my first socket A/Duron 1300 system (Windows 98se) and this one going. I started playing with Linux in Late 2003, not really getting the hang of it for about a year, then Ubuntu came along. I never really  looked back. I have 2 newer systems with dual core AM2 CPU's and Raptor hard drives, they just don't get my attention as much.

SirShaggy

### Post by MeduZa on 2008-06-08
> **Unix_Slayer said:**
> Don't ever worry that antifreeze will hit the board? I've got great cooling in my system. Never really goes over 85 degrees. How low does the temp go down with the water cooling?

4 months ago that happened, all the northbridge and the sound card (pci ports includes) get filled of ugly, smelly green liquid, the coolant is non corrosive and non conductive, nothing happened, I cleaned and exchanged the broken block (this cooland destroyed the acrylic block :S )

Liquid hybrid (like my) system only down a little the temp no too much (compared with air or pasive cooling), the good is, no noise, and the temp don't move when the system start generating head, from IDLE: 32°c (87°F) to FULL: 53°c (127.40°F) and changes sloooooly, water is a heat regulator.

My sensors are:
normal use:
Core0 Temp:  +87.8°F < read from the CPU
Core1 Temp:  +91.4°F < read from the CPU

VCore 1:     +1.14 V
fan1:       2311 RPM < fans are LOW (no noise)
fan2:        992 RPM  < fans are LOW (no noise)
fan3:       5625 RPM < this is a 1 1/2 inch fan on the memory
M/B Temp:    +95.0°F < shouthbridge
CPU Temp:   +100.2°F < read from the Motherboard
Chipset:     +75.2°F < northbridge

GPU: 109.4 ºF

I use Celsius:

Core0 Temp:  +32.0°C                                    
Core1 Temp:  +35.0°C                                    

VCore 1:     +1.14 V
fan1:       2311 RPM
fan2:        992 RPM
fan3:       5625 RPM
M/B Temp:    +36.0°C 
CPU Temp:    +39.0°C 
Chipset:     +25.0°C

---

You will now become part of the Mail Order business. In this business your product is not solid and tangible, it's a service. You are in the business of developing Mailing Lists. Many large corporations are happy to pay big bucks for quality lists. However, the money made from the mailing lists is secondary to the income which is made from people like you and me asking to be included in that list. Here are the 4 easy steps to success: STEP 1: Get 6 separate pieces of paper and write the following on each piece of paper;PLEASE PUT ME ON YOUR MAILING LIST. Now get 6 US $1.00 bills and place ONE inside EACH of the 6 pieces of paper so the bill will not be seen through the envelope (to prevent thievery). Next, place one paper in each of the 6 envelopes and seal them. You should now have 6 sealed envelopes, each with a piece of paper stating the above phrase, your name and address, and a $1.00 bill. What you are doing is creating a service. THIS IS ABSOLUTELY LEGAL! You are requesting a legitimate service and you are paying for it! Like most of us I was a little skeptical and a little worried about the legal aspects of it all. So I checked it out with the U.S. Post Office (1-800-725-2161) and they confirmed that it is indeed legal! Mail the 6 envelopes to the following addresses:  1) Jordan Savoy 4549 North 16 street. Philadelphia PA 19140 =2) Matt Madracki P.O. Box 1529 Sutton West, Ontario L0E 1R0 =3) Esan Brown 2006 SCHENECTADY AVENUE BROOKLYN,NY 11234 =4) Andre Harris 56 Sheridan Ave. APT 4f Mount Vernon, NY 10552 =5) Valdimer Gryntoski 2105 Neals Lane Vancouver WA 98661 =6) Omar Salvio  P.O. Box 16578 Riyadh, KSA 11474   STEP 2: Now take the =1 name off the list that you see above, move the other names up (6 becomes 5, 5 becomes 4, etc...) and add YOUR Name as number 6 on the list. STEP 3: Change anything you need to, but try to keep this article as close to original as possible. Now, post your amended article to at least 200 newsgroups. (I think there are close to 24,000 groups) All you need is 200, but remember, the more you post, the more money you make! This is perfectly legal! If you have any doubts, refer to Title 18 Sec. 1302 & 1341 of the Postal lottery laws. Keep a copy of these steps for yourself and, whenever you need money, you can use it again, and again. PLEASE REMEMBER that this program remains successful because of the honesty and integrity of the participants and by their carefully adhering to the directions. Look at it this way. If you are of integrity, the program will continue and the money that so many others have received will come your way. NOTE: You may want to retain every name and address sent to you, either on a computer or hard copy and keep the notes people send you. This VERIFIES that you are truly providing a service. (Also, it might be a good idea to wrap the $1 bill in dark paper to reduce the risk of mail theft.) So, as each post is downloaded and the directions carefully followed, six members will be reimbursed for their participation as a List Developer with one dollar each. Your name will move up the list geometrically so that when your name reaches the =1 position you will be receiving thousands of dollars in CASH!!! What an opportunity for only $6.00 ($1.00 for each of the first six people listed above) Send it now, add your own name to the list and you're in business! ---DIRECTIONS ----- FOR HOW TO POST TO NEWSGROUPS------------ Step 1) You do not need to re-type this entire letter to do your own posting. Simply put your cursor at the beginning of this letter and drag your cursor to the bottom of this document, and select 'copy' from the edit menu. This will copy the entire letter into the computer's memory. Step 2) Open a blank 'notepad' file and place your cursor at the top of the blank page. From the 'edit' menu select 'paste'. This will paste a copy of the letter into notepad so that you can add your name to the list. Step 3) Save your new notepad file as a .txt file. If you want to do your postings in different settings, you'll always have this file to go back to. Step 4) Use Netscape or Internet explorer and try searching for various newsgroups (on-line forums, message boards, chat sites, discussions.) Step 5) Visit these message boards and post this article as a new message by highlighting the text of this letter and selecting paste from the edit menu. Fill in the Subject, this will be the header that everyone sees as they scroll through the list of postings in a particular group, click the post message button. You're done with your first one! Congratulations...THAT'S IT! All you have to do is jump to different newsgroups and post away, after you get the hang of it, it will take about 30 seconds for each newsgroup! **REMEMBER, THE MORE NEWSGROUPS YOU POST IN, THE MORE MONEY YOU WILL MAKE!! BUT YOU HAVE TO POST A MINIMUM OF 200** That's it! You will begin receiving money from around the world within days! You may eventually want to rent a P.O.Box due to the large amount of mail you will receive. If you wish to stay anonymous, you can invent a name to use, as long as the postman will deliver it. **JUST MAKE SURE ALL THE ADDRESSES ARE CORRECT.** Now the WHY part: Out of 200 postings, say I receive only 5 replies (a very low example). So then I made $5.00 with my name at =6 on the letter. Now, each of the 5 persons who just sent me $1.00 make the MINIMUM 200 postings, each with my name at =5 and only 5 persons respond to each of the original 5, that is another $25.00 for me, now those 25 each make 200 MINIMUM posts with my name at =4 and only 5 replies each, I will bring in an additional $125.00! Now, those 125 persons turn around and post the MINIMUM 200 with my name at =3 and only receive 5 replies each, I will make an additional $626.00! OK, now here is the fun part, each of those 625 persons post a MINIMUM 200 letters with my name at =2 and they each only receive 5 replies, that just made me $3,125.00!!! Those 3,125 persons will all deliver this message to 200 newsgroups with my name at =1 and if still 5 persons per 200 newsgroups react I will receive $15,625,00! With an original investment of only $6.00! AMAZING! When your name is no longer on the list, you just take the latest posting in the newsgroups, and send out another $6.00 to names on the list, putting your name at number 6 again. And start posting again. The thing to remember is: do you realize that thousands of people all over the world are joining the internet and reading these articles everyday?, JUST LIKE YOU are now!! So, can you afford $6.00 and see if it really works?? I think so... People have said, "e;what if the plan is played out and no one sends you the money? So what! What are the chances of that happening when there are tons of new honest users and new honest people who are joining the internet and newsgroups everyday and are willing to give it a try? Estimates are at 20,000 to 50,000 new users, every day, with thousands of those joining the actual internet. Remember: play FAIRLY and HONESTLY and this will really work.

### Post by crazyness003 on 2008-08-19
[COLOR=Red]**I want to apologize if this has already been posted. If so, someone do a merge or something, because i couldn't find anything like this.**[/COLOR]

Okay, so this is simple:
There a poll asking for the processor you use. Please use your current machine (the one you are using to make the post...if you own it). I know some, if not, most people have multiple machines with different configs. So just keep it simple.

Then after you take the poll, give us a list of the hardware (if custom built) or the name and model of your rig (if you can list some hardware about it too).

If you have multiple machines, post all of them. Separate them in a coordinated manner.

Some pics would be nice and links to the exact model you have (if you bought it pre-built).
And if possible, post the pics as attachments, they take up less space on the thread, its just neater. But again, use your own discretion. 

Please keep it clean, not too wordy (I dont care if you give us a little histroy, its nice to have some backstory), and dont tell people they suck just because they use Intel...ahem:rolleyes:.

Hopefully this benefits the community somehow.

On top of everything you now have a place to link your signature stats to. Just post your stats here, click on the POST # of your stat, copy the URL of your **single** post and insert this text in your signature (in the User CP)
```
[noparse][My Stats (or whatever you wish)]("http://the url you just copied")[/noparse]
```This is what i did (see my sig)

### Post by crazyness003 on 2008-10-31

I have to apologize to the powerPC users. I completely forgot about that architecture. But i guess its part of others. Also [SPARC]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SPARC")

### Post by Capt. Mac on 2008-11-25
Currently my only machine is a [Linux Certified LC2100s]("http://linuxcertified.com/linux-laptop-lc2100s.html") notebook.

Specs:

Hard Drive: Hitachi HTS54251 ATA Disk 120 GB (I only ordered 100 GB :))
RAM: 2 GB
Processor: Intel Core 2 Duo T7100 1.80 Ghz
Graphics: Intel GM965/GL960 Integrated Graphics Controller
Audio: Intel 82801H (ICH8 Family) HD Audio Controller
Wireless: Intel PRO/Wireless 3945ABG Network Connection
Optical: QSI DVD+-RW SDW-085
Battery: Lithium Ion Battery (32560mWh capacity, voltage=14.8V)

Overall, I am very satisfied, they gave me more than I paid for (bigger hard disk, dvd burner should have cost extra). Battery life leaves a little to be desired, but I'm never far from an outlet anyhow so that's a moot point for me. I have nothing but good things to say about this product. Intel GM965 is blacklisted for Compiz, but it has worked perfectly since day 1.

### Post by Rumbl3 on 2009-03-04
in my sig. 

Working on a custom water block at work right now. So that should be in by the end of the week cause we have cnc's at work hehe. So we can make stuff better then what you can buy :).

plus the copper only cost me 10 bucks for some spare copper we had around the shop machine time is free after hours. 

We already made one for my buddy works awesome. The prototype were making for me has copper pins that come straight threw to the cpu so they make direct contact with the cpu to allow heat to escape faster. Also no flow restriction which is the biggest problem with store bought water blocks :) 
OWNAGE 

yeah i know older machine but i wouldn't be making it if it wasn't for having the equipment infront of me to make it for next to nothing.

### Post by Unanimated on 2009-03-22
Alright, here's my (extremely) outdated gaming PC.

**Default:**
CPU: AMD Athlon XP+ 2000
RAM: 755.6 MB
HDD: 60GB Western Digital (primary), 30GB Maxtor (secondary), 20GB Maxtor (unpowered secondary)
CD-ROM: Generic Samsung, maybe Sony CD-ROM drive (replacement for broken one)
CD-RW: HP CD-Writer Plus
Video Card: NVIDIA GeForce 6200
Sound Card: Creative Labs SB Audigy
FireWire: Creative Labs SB Audigy FireWire port (...what? Why is there a FireWire port on a sound card?)
Alternate, Unused Sound Card: Integrated with MSI motherboard
Ethernet Controller: ADMTek NC100 Network Everywhere Fast Ethernet 10/100 (not used for now)
Speakers: Genica Hi-Fi Speakers
Mouse: Logitech M-BJ58/M-BJ69 Optical Wheel Mouse
Gamepads: Logitech Dual Action Gamepad, Interact ProPad 8 Digital
Keyboard: Logitech Access Keyboard
Backup Keyboard: Dynex USB Internet Keyboard

Yeah, it was a good gaming computer... About 5-6 years ago.. Oh well, it plays OpenArena, Tremulous, and Urban Terror, all with maxed out graphics (:

**$453 Computer to Save For:**
CPU: Intel Celeron Dual Core 2 GHz
RAM: 4 1GB RAM Sticks (4GB in all)
HDD: 1.5 Terabyte Seagate Barracuda (primary), 320 GB Western Digital Caviar (secondary), maybe more but that depends on the HD connections with CD drives
DVD-RW: Sony DVD/CD Rewritable IDE Retail Drive
Video Card: NVIDIA GeForce 9600 (can't wait!!)
Sound Card: Onboard motherboard sound, but possibly a Creative Labs Sound Blaster Audigy, but that's probably not worth the trouble
FireWire: N/A
Ethernet Controller: Same as in meh old PC, what's the difference?
Wireless Access: Netgear USB 2.0 Wireless Adapter
Speakers: Logitech USB Digital Speakers
Mouse: Same, but I'll eventually get a new one (:
Keyboard: Same, I love this keyboard
Gamepads: Same
Motherboard: ASUS P5QL PRO Motherboard (again, can't wait)

It's hard to believe that I'm going to get that awesome PC for $453... That's just a little more than the XBOX I'm saving for (:

Oh, operating systems:
**Default and new PC:**
Kubuntu 8.10--->
MAYBE Windows XP, it gets the Blue Screen of Death when I try to install it on my default PC :(
And no, it's after the disc part - usually when it gets to Installing Devices... it gets the screen. I mainly just tried to install it for Quake LIVE and other games, then I realized there's no point, so I formatted the drive I was installing Windows on back to ext3 and now it's a backup drive for my Kubuntu 8.10 again.

EDIT: I would dual boot between Kubuntu and Windows, if I ever installed Windows again.

Browsers:
**Default and new PC:**
Firefox. Always. Except sometimes I decide to randomly switch to Opera, and then go back to Firefox after a few days (one time it was a mere hour o_O)

Anyway, if you see any possible Linux-related issues with the hardware on the new PC (I'm mainly wondering about the USB speakers..), PLEASE PM me. I'd greatly appreciate it.

### Post by mattlach on 2009-07-29
Alright.  I'll bite...

I just updated my computer after using the same box for over 5 years.

Formerly a HUGE AMD fan, I have now come to realize that intel momentarily have the lead.

My recently retired system was an AMD Athlon64 2000+ (Socket 754) on an Nforce3 board.   One of the primary drivers to upgrade was nvidias lack of support for the nforce3 chipset.

What I upgraded to now:

[[img]http://farm3.static.flickr.com/2607/3702488800_41b04d1f33_o.jpg[/img]](http://www.flickr.com/photos/mattlach/3702488800/)

(It's weird to see 8 logical CPU's up there)

I got one of those compact shuttle cases designed for the new Intel Core i7 architecture.   I got the cheapest one, the 920.  Eventually I will overclock it, but I haven't gotten around to it yet.

The ram is triple channel, so I have 6GB of ram (a 1600 2 gig stick on each channel)

So its a quad core CPU, with hyperthreading, so each core acts as two logical processors.

I didn't bother with a fancy graphics card, as I don't play games anymore, so I got one of the budget Nvidia cards, the 9400GT.

And - of course - I'm running it on 64bit Ubuntu :p

Thats mine.  It works pretty well for what I do.

### Post by ShakeyJake on 2010-01-17
Just done a round of upgrading as I've gotten back into computers again.

Desktop:
AMD Phenom II 955 (4x 3.2GHz with a Scythe Shuriken HSF)
4GB Corsair Dominator DDR3
Gainward 1GB 9500GT Passive
MSI 770-C45
OCZ Vertex Turbo 32GB SSD (for /)
WD Green 1.5TB (for /home)
LG DVD Writer
Antec 300 (with a Corsair CX400 and a load of Scythe Golfball fans)

This runs into 2x Samsung Syncmasters at 1680x1050 each to pull stuff out of it, with a Saitek Eclipse II and a Logitech MX mouse to punch stuff in. As for the stuff itself, that's Ubuntu 9.10 64-bit.


Server:
AMD Athlon 4600+ (2x 2.4GHz)
4GB OCZ Platinum DDR2
Zotac 8200-C-E
Patriot 32GB SSD (for /, though this may be changed for a normal laptop drive very soon)
WD Green 1.5TB (for /home)

This is plugged into a Sharp TV and a cheapo keyboard and mouse, though 99% of the time it's just left running effectively headless. Ubuntu 9.10 64-bit.


Laptop:
Asus Eee PC 900
900MHz Celeron
1GB DDR2
4GB(/)+16GB(/home) SSDs
Ubuntu 9.04 32-bit.


Beigetrosity:
AMD Athlon Somethingorother (1.6GHz)
1.5GB DDR
An Asus Motherboard
Hitachi Deathstar 40GB HDD
Big Ugly(tm) Beige Case
2xDVD Drives, 2x Floppy Drives

Used to be the server, currently unused. Ubuntu Server 8.04 LTS. Might make this a Windows computer, it runs the games I actually play (Theme Hospital, Dungeon Keeper et al) very well.


And my old desktop now belongs to my family as an HTPC. But that was largely cannibalised and I have no idea what components it now sports.

### Post by ZarathustraDK on 2010-02-06
Oh gee, I got a mediacenter, a desktop, an Eee 901, 2 old desktops in need of being refurnished, and a broken old IBM laptop, all run/ran Ubuntu.

Processor: AMD Athlon X2 4850e 2.5 GHz dualcore
RAM: 4GB generic Kingston 800MHz
Motherboard: Asus M3N78 PRO
Graphics: Nvidia 9800GT (ok that aint green, but I want fluid playback)
Harddisks: 1x 74GB WD VelociRaptor (for systempartition), 3x 1TB WD green HD's for storage (idles at about 4W)
Monitor: Samsung UE46B6000 46" LED-TV

Just a buyers note: The Samsung UE46B6000 rocks, so get it while you can if you're in the market for one. Later versions of their LED-TV's got a lot of superfluous features like builtin youtube and network-cabling, pretty useless if you're gonna hook it up to a computer anyway, however those features still prop up the price.

> **ubunterooster said:**
> Athlon x2 225 (either @ .8 or 4 GHZ at any given time)
8Gb DDR2 RAM (@400 or 1445 MHZ)
128GB Torq SSD
6TB various other drives

Dont believe the rated MHz on DDR. To make it sound a lot faster than normal SD RAM, they lied. DDR1 (and all the other types of DDR) use 'doubled' MHz, so 1445MHz is probably running at 722.5MHz. 

You can check that with lshw, it (should) tell you the real data rate.

### Post by alanmoore78 on 2010-09-19
My laptop is an Acer Extensa 5230E, which is to say when it came out of the box it was saddled with 2GB of RAM, a 160GB HD, Windows 7 Home Premium 64-bit, and a 2.2GHZ Celeron 900 processor.  It was painful.  I have upgraded since and now have a 2.1GHz Pentium Dual Core T4300 processor and 3GB of memory.  I must say the best upgrade yet was moving from 7HP64 to Ubuntu 10.04 AMD64.

I have been running this for close to 24 hours straight with one reboot to install the first set of update packages and my only concerns now are minor.  My CPU temp is staying in the high 50Cs.  I can cool it to 47C by placing a fan directly at the bottom of the laptop for a few minutes.  But 57C-58C is typical.  Compare this to the 67C I was pulling at idle and the 72C I got under load in Windows.  Windows throttled CPU output because it kept getting so hot.  No such thing happening now, but I will watch the little AWM dock and if it goes over 67C.  I'm tempted to get a little USB-powered fan and tape it under the laptop to force air into the case.

---

Had to disable IDE interface to CDRom (old CDRom hardware) would hang the installation trying to initialize the CDRom.
